A Timeless Sacred Experience at Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary, Peru If you’re chasing awe-inspiring scenery woven with ancient wonder, Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a must-see. This UNESCO-listed wonder blends breathtaking Andean peaks, remarkable Inca architecture, and a sense of sacred mystery that lingers long after your visit. What makes it special Iconic, panoramic setting: Nestled high in the Andes, the terraced citadel rises against dramatic skies, offering vistas that feel almost otherworldly and utterly unforgettable. Architectural ingenuity: The precision of Inca stonework, the sun temples, and the agricultural terraces showcase sophisticated engineering and aesthetic harmony with the natural landscape. Rich historical context: The sanctuary anchors a wealth of stories about Inca civilization, daily life, astronomy, and ritual practice, inviting exploration and imagination. Spiritual resonance: For many visitors, the site carries a contemplative energy—an invitation to pause, reflect, and connect with history, culture, and nature. Cultural and natural blend: The combination of rocky outcrops, cloud-draped ridges, and well-preserved ruins creates a holistic experience of humanity’s ancient ingenuity in harmony with the environment. What to expect A multi-sensory journey: The textures of stone, the scent of the highland air, and the expansive views create a memorable, immersive adventure. Guided and self-guided insights: Local guides offer fascinating context, while well-marked trails allow for independent exploration at your own pace. Photographic opportunities everywhere: Every terrace, stair, and stonework promises a picture-perfect moment against the dramatic Andean backdrop. Practical tips Plan ahead for altitude and weather: The high elevation can be challenging; hydrate, pace yourself, and dress in layers. Wear sturdy footwear: Expect uneven paths, steps, and some steep sections. Start early or late: To beat crowds and catch the golden light, consider early morning or late afternoon visits. Respect local customs and preservation rules: Stay on designated paths, do not touch ancient walls, and follow guidance to protect this sacred site. Bottom line Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a monumental, life-changing destination that blends natural beauty, architectural prowess, and cultural heritage into a once-in-a-lifetime experience. It’s a must-visit for travelers seeking inspiration, historical insight, and a powerful reminder of humanity’s enduring curiosity and resilience. Highly recommended for history buffs, hikers, photographers, and anyone who wants to connect with a sacred echo of the past.

70 Soles ($22 USD) Foreigner Entrance Fee. Here you will be able to see governments continue to separate humans by ethnicity in the form of an Entrance Pass. The big issue with Saqsaywaman is the park does not offer a Single Entrance Pass. Your entrance fee is buying 4 Park Passes valid for One Day Only! I was on a bicycle riding around the World. It was around 2.30pm when I visited, so I would not be able to take advantage of seeing any other parks today. The park staff said I could buy a more expensive 10 Day Pass. There are No Informational Signs with information other than a name as you tour the park. I visited on a Sunday, and Saqsaywaman feels more like a place you come to play in the afternoon with your family. Kids and adults were running and climbing all over the "ruins". The Park Staff didn't know where I should store the bicycle while I visited the park. The Park Staff suggested parking the bicycle in the parking lot with the cars. Don't look for the park staff to welcome you, or assist your experience of Saqsaywaman in any way. Saqsaywaman has a nice overlook of Cusco. Or you can visit the Statue of Christ or the San Cristobal Church for similar overlooks of Cusco if you do not wish to buy into the 4 Park Pass scheme.

I decided to take the Spanish tour with my limited Spanish. I read this place up in Wiki and a few other websites night before. It is a cool place and had a great view on the top. I was thinking where were they before? Maybe night tour is much more popular, and all of them were gearing towards the night tour. Overall, I was disappointed at the experience.

Nice dining at the historical place. The food was good. Well cooked, plated, and served. The taste was exotic. We tried dishes made of local ingredients. Quinoa cheese ball was good. I liked it. The Guinea pig appetizer was so-so. The fatty part was little too chewy for me. If you like fried fatty food you may disagree on that. The fish was nice. I thought if the fish meat was little thinner, it would have tasted better with the quinoa crust. The green bean sauce was nice but again little too blend. I think it's a good place to try out. Staffs are friendly and saw many locals. Once was probably good for us travelling people.

Incredible experience! Our guide, Jordan, was knowledgeable, and really tailored his tour to our interests. We took the Inca Bridge option, and was so pleased with how little people were on the trail. We got to see more of the orchids, insects ( especially the butterflies) and hummingbirds up close. The city of Machu Pichu was really impressive! At first it seemed like a lot of people, but by the time we got back from the Inca Bridge, most of them were gone. So it was not crowded. We were able to get stunning photos even in the low season! It did rain closer to the end of our tour, but not a lot, and we were prepared for it. Bring a rain jacket, and some waterproof pants, if you can. A hat also helps. That's if you're going during the rainy season. No umbrellas or selfie sticks are allowed.

It is a long (but not particularly dangerous) drive there and back, particularly if the tour bus makes stops at the hot springs. Wonderfull and beautifull ruins from the old inkas. One of the world wonders. If you go to Perú you have to visit Machu Picchu, it´s a must. Machu Picchu Is undoubtedly one of the most incredible places we've ever been too, helped by good weather and a quieter day with fewer people. However, there's a few things though that could be done to improve the experience, particularly given the hefty price tag attached to the visit. - There is zero information around the site about it's history or design. When visiting one of the wonders of the world it seems insane that zero information is provided to let people learn more about the sites rich past. I presume this is to encourage the use of guides, but regardless a small number of boards, even just indicating what certain spaces are or what they were used for, should be a minimum. - Toilets should be free. Given the amount it costs, there is no excuse for charging for the toilets at the top of the hill. If required, get people to show their tickets before being able to use the bathroom, but to outright charge is just a desperate attempt to squeeze you for a couple more dollars

The lines were amazing to see, the flight less enjoyable as it was tourning a lot. Three out of five passagers got sick... Unfortunately, our experience visiting Machu Picchu did not meet our expectations. First, one must take a train to get there. The ticket prices are quite high, yet the trains are old and unreliable. Our departure was delayed by an hour because the train broke down en route. The train stations are small and cannot accommodate the number of people waiting, forcing many to sit on the floor or stand until boarding. Moreover, the overpriced buses that take you to the Machu Picchu entrance are also outdated and in desperate need of replacement. My seat was broken, making it impossible to lean back comfortably. The greatest disappointment, however, was Machu Picchu itself. It was difficult to obtain Circus 2 tickets to enter the ancient city. Once we arrived, we found that many important and interesting sites were closed to visitors, a fact that was not disclosed when we paid the full price. Overall, it was a disappointing experience. I wonder where all the money goes, as conditions appear to worsen each year.

I’m giving a 1-star review for the absolutely terrible new ticketing system. Forcing visitors to see only one tiny section of Machu Picchu is a horrendous idea, and if other world wonders followed this model, world tourism would collapse. My experience was a complete disaster. I originally had a ticket for Circuit No. 2 and wanted to go to the top, but my official tour guide insisted the top was part of Circuit No. 1. So I purchased an additional ticket for Circuit No. 1 just to make sure I could see everything. Then, when I tried to go up, the staff told me that what I wanted to see was not Ticket No. 1 but it was actually Circuit No. 2, and because my original Circuit No. 2 ticket had been valid in the morning, it was now “expired”. So despite buying different tickets, they still refused to let me see the top of circuit 2 from Machu Picchu. Their only response was to tell me to complain to my tour guide, which is absolutely useless and does nothing to fix the fact that I ended up out of one important part I came to see. What makes this even worse is their complete lack of empathy. They don’t understand, or probably don’t care, that people wait months or even years to visit Machu Picchu, and for many it’s a once-in-a-lifetime experience. If someone has a VALID ticket, even if it expired, DURING LOW SEASONS, a few hours earlier due to confusing instructions, the staff should show some humanity and let them in. There is absolutely no reason to be so strict. It was even late afternoon and there wasn't any crowd at all. Instead, the rangers and gate agents were rude, unhelpful, inflexible, and completely indifferent. They just recite the same memorized line "your ticket expired" and refuse to help in any real way. Honestly, AI robots would do a far better job than the staff currently working there. I had heard many complaints about Machu Picchu, but now I've experienced this myself. Hope this review helps someone
Surprise package of the city. Amazing fountains. Great to go with family and kids. Take shorts and prepare to get wet. Fantastic !!

Highly recommend this beautiful park with “magical” fountains. Impressed by the quality of the park with such an affordable entrance fee. Kids loved the little amusement park with bouncy house slides, bungee-jumping trampoline and little boats. We enjoyed the laser light show projected on the main fountain. Very meager food choices so be prepared.

I really enjoyed walking around these ruins. It had a great view of Cusco too. We originally were going to walk up to the ruins from Cusco but glad we didn't as it was very uphill. I wish we would've known about the local bus instead of taking a more expensive taxi that stops at Saqsaywaman and goes all the way to Tambomachay for 1 soles. Also note you cannot pay for just Saqsaywaman, you have to buy either a pass for 4 ruins for 70 soles or the big one for 150 soles. The other 3 ruins are past Saqsaywaman and in my opinion weren't really that great, but worth it if you catch the local bus for 1 soles since they're included in the 70 soles pass. We spent 2-3 hours at Saqsaywaman without a guide and the other ruins probably 2 hours with transportation so it makes for a nice day.

Me encantó el lugar, en este mes de febrero dicen que es temporada baja por las lluvias, pero yo igual recomendaría el viaje porque todo está más barato, además, que más puedo pedir, Kuelap me recibió con un sol imponente.. nunca lo olvidaré..

Nice walking place for dollar and half. U should go by 7pm when light show is running. They also have slash water for kids during summer months. U should respect grass on don't sit on unassigned areas

Such a beautiful park, I Kept walking past my park as I was walking around the city & every time I looked through & saw the fountains I thought I must go but nothing quite prepared me for just how beautiful it was. I highly recommend spending the day into the evening here so you get the full daylight & evening experience. Do make sure your phone is fully charged as you may want to take 1000 photos 🤣! Enjoy.

En el Museo de Sitio Chan Chan, que está muy cerca del complejo arqueológico “Chan Chan”, se encuentran resguardadas y exhibidas ​muchas piezas arqueológicas de cerámica, piezas líticas y de metales, textiles y restos óseos provenientes del complejo arqueológico y sus alrededores. Una de las cosas más interesantes para apreciar en el museo es una enorme maqueta que recrea las posibles dimensiones de la ciudad de “Chan Chan”, fue una ciudad precolombina de adobe, construida cerca a la costa por los chimúes. También es la ciudad construida en adobe más grande de América​ y la segunda en el mundo.

Pésimo lugar muy mal descuidado no tiene señalización ni nadie que te oriente almenos por el cobro de la entrada y todo allá es gratis cuál es la gracia de pagar si todo está en campo abierto para todos

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Conocer nuestra historia, siempre será una experiencia única y mejor lugar para saber mucho más de la cultura Mochica y su alta sociedad Acordarse que los días Lunes no hay atención; las personas de tercera edad no pagan; en la parte atrás del museo hay algunas artesanías de Chiclayo, supermercados recomendados; por último adquirir algún guía, son atentos y explican toda la historia.

El museo de sitio es sencillo pero claro y conciso con datos claves y diagramas informativos bien explicados. El recorrido moderadamente extenso de las diversas ruinas del Santuario se puede hacer a pie, en bicicleta o en movilidad propia. Lamentablemente algunos detalles importantes de las ruinas no se pueden ver directamente si no desde una distancia considerable. Y se entiende el acceso restringido porque es lamentable ver en algunas zonas graffitis, grabados y otros atentados hechos por personas sin escrúpulos ni cultura que han deteriorado severamente éste patrimonio histórico y cultural de la humanidad.

The place is really nice and big but you can't enter most of it. You are only allowed in a kind of street together with cars. There are way too many small areas closed for no reason and no explanation, you can definitely see that there is a clear way to access with even footprints. Guards are standing in areas restricted for visitors and most of the time even standing on ruins whistling at you if you accidentally step on the sand out of the way. It's still nice to go there for a walk but the experience in general is not that far from looking at pictures in a museum.

If you’ve been to any other Incan Ruins or done Machu Picchu this is not worth a visit. It’s one hour guided tour (no self touring allowed), walks through ruins within the city. Recommend other Inca sites, however it’s a reasonable way to burn an hour or so. Entry approx 15 soles

destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Huacas del Sol y Luna y Museo de Sitio son parte del complejo arqueológico ubicado en la campiña de Moche al sur este de la ciudad de Trujillo. Es uno de los lugares turísticos más visitados dentro de la denominada Ruta Moche ... En este lugar se puede observar (in situ) durante su ameno recorrido de promedio hora y media, gracias al aporte de los guías se nos indica dónde estuvo ubicada la capital de esta cultura pre inca, cede del gobierno politico, militar y espiritual desarrollada entre los 100 y 600 años DC ... Dentro de su espectacular recorrido se puede observar en los 5 niveles excavados los murales construidos con adobes conservando sus colores originales, destacan su construcción piramidal y el Apaiec (degollador), además conocemos de su cultura, etnia y gastronomía. El recorrido de la Ruta Moche incluye las visitas al Complejo Arqueológico y Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan (ciudadela de barro más grande del mundo), Huaca Esmeralda, Huaca Arco Iris, Huaca y Museo de Sitio de la Señora de Cao ... El costo de ingreso para cada lugar indicado es: adultos S/. 10 soles, universitarios con carné a la vista S/. 5 soles y escolares S/. 1 sol.
Este sitio arqueológico esta en un proceso de refacción , pero aún así la vista desde alli es espectacular, muy interesante la cultura que lo respalda

Es el mejor lugar del Perú paisajisticamente hablando, tiene lagunas, cascadas, montañas nevadas, micro climas con farallones de roca de granito de mas de 800 metros de altura, en la parte norte entre YUNGAY y CARAZ, se concentra lo mas importante y bonito de todo el parque y/o CORDILLERA BLANCA..como el nevado Huascaran (6,768 m)el mas alto del Perú, lagunas de Llanganuco, lago Parón rodeado de montañas entre ellas el Artezonraju q es símbolo de la PARAMOUNT PICTURE, El Alpamayo. Considerada la montaña mas bella del mundo, El trekking de Santa Cruz, etc.

BAD EXPERIENCE with GUIDE in the entrance of the archeological side. There is no fixed price, you shouldn't trust the price that they are suggesting and you always have to bargain. He said that he did the "percurso medio" but he did the short one and made us pay 100 soles in total after 45 min. If we remember well his name was Sebastian. We felt like we were scammed. It is really pity because all the archeological sites in the sacred valley are really interesting as well as the Ollantaytambo but it is a continuous burgeon and there are no fixed guide prices. It is really pity!

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: "If you're looking for a unique and unforgettable experience in Peru, I highly recommend visiting the Nasca Plateau. The expansive desert landscape is truly awe-inspiring, and the ancient lines etched into the ground will leave you in wonder. It's a testament to the ingenuity and skill of the Nasca people who created them so long ago. The best way to fully appreciate the magnitude and intricacy of the lines is from above, with a scenic flight. The pilots are knowledgeable and provide fascinating insights into the history, culture, and mystery surrounding the lines. Overall, the Nasca Plateau is a must-visit destination for anyone interested in ancient civilizations and mysteries of the world."
Great experience at night...something different and so many fountains and colors...great value and so cheap to enter @$2...well worth it...

Visiting Machu Picchu is an amazing adventure. There are a few ways to get into the top of the mountain: you can walk from Machu Picchu Pueblo or you can take a bus from the center of the Pueblo (need to buy the tickets from a local bus company Conset Tour - the only one that does this driving, around 24 USD the two-way). And to access the ruins you also need to buy the tickets, you can book them online or buy them on the local Peru tourist station. There are 4 different circuits that you need to choose to enter the ruins, which give access to different parts of Machu Picchu.

Pucllana Site Museum in Miraflores offers a fascinating glimpse into Lima’s pre-Incan history. The guided tours, included in the affordable entry fee, take you through the adobe pyramid, small museum exhibits, and a native flora and fauna area in about an hour. Its central location makes it easy to visit, and the contrast of ancient ruins amid the modern city is striking. While the site is historically significant, it’s relatively small and not as visually impressive as other Peruvian ruins. A worthwhile and educational stop if you’re nearby, but not a must-see if you’re short on time.

It's Machu Picchu, what else is there to say? Beautiful photo locations, great place to see llamas up close. (A local worker informed me they were llamas, and not Alpacas) We did not use a guide service as my wife and I like the solo travel style, but from what I saw the guides were quite knowledgeable and helpful to their tour groups. Questions we had before going there I'll try to answer; 1. What should we wear? We ended up wearing casual hiking clothes. I wore a tank top, t-shirt, into hoodie with just normal pants on the bottom with work/hiking boots. My wife wore a full winter layer setup as she gets cold easily. We both had to take our top layers off as we were in the sun and got hot. Other people looked to be wearing full blown hiking gear (maybe they were doing the Gran Caverna hike or ascending to Machu Picchu without a bus) while others wore more fashionable choices for photos. Honestly the weather will decide it for it and usually mountain climates are unpredictable! 2. Which circuit should we take? We took circuit 3 and found it very nice. Can't speak much for the other two, but from what we saw online circuit 3 had the best views. 3. How can we get a bus ticket in advance? We can't answer that, we ended up buying our ticket in the office the day before our trip at the office. The tickets are for anytime, but are linked to your passport so be sure to bring it to when you buy your ticket(s). Also when you line up for the bus, you'll be organized by your entrance ticket time into Machu Picchu. The bus ride was maybe ~20 minutes, and they send a few at a time up and down. Seems frequent enough. Final Comments There is a toilet at the top but not inside the sanctuary itself. Be sure to go before you go! It's 2 Soles to use the restroom. I found the snack bar at the top to be quite nice and affordable, which is abnormal for such a touristy place. So that was nice! Pack water! Stay hydrated!

Aquí tienes un texto de 300 palabras sobre el Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan 🏺✨: --- Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan está ubicado cerca del complejo arqueológico de Chan Chan, en el distrito de Huanchaco, provincia de Trujillo, región La Libertad – Perú. Su creación tuvo como objetivo preservar, investigar y difundir la riqueza cultural del Reino Chimú, una de las civilizaciones preincaicas más importantes de la costa norte peruana. Este museo permite a los visitantes comprender mejor la historia, el arte y la organización social de la antigua ciudad de Chan Chan. El museo fue inaugurado en 2006 y cuenta con salas de exposición permanentes donde se muestran piezas arqueológicas halladas en las excavaciones del sitio. Entre los objetos más destacados se encuentran cerámicas, herramientas, utensilios domésticos, textiles, objetos de madera y metales preciosos. También se exhiben maquetas que reconstruyen la forma original de la ciudad, ayudando a los visitantes a imaginar cómo vivían los chimúes. Una de las particularidades del museo es su diseño arquitectónico, que está inspirado en las estructuras originales de Chan Chan. Sus muros de adobe evocan el estilo constructivo chimú, logrando una armonía visual con el entorno arqueológico. Además, el museo ofrece paneles informativos, mapas y material audiovisual que explican la historia de la cultura Chimú, su relación con el mar, su economía agrícola y su organización política jerarquizada. El Museo de Sitio de Chan Chan cumple un rol fundamental en la conservación del patrimonio cultural peruano. No solo protege y exhibe valiosas piezas arqueológicas, sino que también promueve la educación y la investigación científica. Es un lugar ideal para estudiantes, turistas nacionales y extranjeros que desean conocer de cerca la grandeza de esta cultura milenaria. Gracias a este museo, la historia de Chan Chan sigue viva y accesible para las futuras generaciones.

Just wow. The views are stunning and history amazing. Make sure to hire a guide. If you came up without one get one at the entrance, it's worth it. There are many details you'll miss otherwise also they happily take pictures of you or your group so no one is ever left out. Best time to go is early morning so there is less people. There are two options, walking up the stairs that can take 1-1.5hrs or a bus (20-25minutes, 12usd ticket one way). I recommend the bus. You'll probably be very tired on Machu Picchu after hiking up there, also there is no view but bushes and stairs on the way up. Either way, if you do plan to hike up then the bridge will be opened around 5am and if you take the bus, first bus leaves 5:30am and buses leave every 5 minutes, people start lining up around 3am. So plan your time well. We went in line 4:05am and got on bus 6:05. We did not save in time wise but definitely did energy wise, we ended up doing 10k steps around Machu Picchu. You can always hike down in the end. Also you can enter twice with one ticket so you can come out to use restroom and eat once and return on the mountain. ALSO down next to toilets is a little table where you can stamp your passport with Machu Picchu stamp, do it, it's beautiful.

Lugar turístico para bañarse y relajarse. Usualmente es medio día de tour, complementado con canotaje en el río mayo por la tarde o castillo de lamas (les recomiendo el primero). En realidad, esta catarata es demasiado concurrida y no hay mucho espacio para colocar las cosas de uno. El agua es helada y refrescante y para poder tomarse fotos debajo de la caída hay que hacer cola debido a la concurrencia de gente. No la recomiendo más que para conocer rápidamente, preferí mucho más el tour a las cataratas de pishurayacu (caminata un poco agotadora de 45 minutos) o las cataratas de huacamaillo (caminata normal de 1 hora y media) por su poca concurrencia de gente.

Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

Probably not worth buying tickets to unless you love ruins and plan to visit them all in a package deal. You can walk up to them easily following Google maps, and don't believe the locals who try to lie to you on your way up. There are only a few who do this but they can be convincing, basically telling you tickets aren't available or the walk is an hour longer. They want to charge you to do what you can essentially do for free with very little effort. I recommend you walk up and decide then if you want to buy the tickets. You'll see the ruins clearly from the ticket both and can make up your mind. If you decide not to, you can always walk to the Cristo instead, which is free and near the ticket booth.

Ollantaytambo is a small, very touristy town and an important Inca site, known for its massive stone terraces and steep steps climbing up the mountainside. It’s believed to have been both a ceremonial centre and a fortress, with impressive stonework and views over the valley. By the time we got there—this being the last stop before taking the train to Aguas Calientes—we were exhausted and didn’t bother going in. Those steps are no joke. We just took a few photos from the bottom and left it at that. The town itself is full of stalls selling similar souvenirs, so it can feel a bit repetitive, but the ruins do stand out, even if you’re just seeing them from below. Martin Boyce Barbados

The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).

Very Nice Park Huascarán National Park (Spanish: Parque Nacional Huascarán) is a Peruvian national park that comprises most of the mountain range known as Cordillera Blanca (the world's highest tropical mountain range) which is part of the central Andes, in the region of Ancash. The park covers an area of 340,000 ha (ca. 3.400 km2) and is managed by the Peruvian Network of Protected Natural Areas: SERNANP (Servicio Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas). It was designated as a World Heritage Site in 1985 by UNESCO, is also a well-known mountaineering spot and harbors a unique biodiversity with plant species such as the Queen of the Andes, trees of the genera Polylepis and Buddleja, and animals such as spectacled bears, condors, vicunas and tarucas. The park is approximately 150 kilometres (93 mi) long from north to south and averages about 25 kilometres (16 mi) in width. The western slope of the Cordillera Blanca drains to the Pacific Ocean via the Santa River and the eastern slopes drain to the Marañon River, and ultimately to the Amazon River and the Atlantic Ocean. destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Muy agradable y bien organizado el museo. Recomendado!!! Y luego a pasear por la plaza de Chiclayo. De la plaza tomas un taxi a 13 soles hasta el museo. El ingreso al museo es 10 soles adulto y a disfrutar el recorrido.

Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

This Inca museum is located in the center of Lima, so much so that the urban planning of the city has been taking away land. We found it a bit monotonous. El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!

Recomiendo hacer el recorrido con un guía para que pueda contarles la historia de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me tocó el Sr. Luis que vive dentro del complejo. También encontrarás dónde comer chicharrones y cecina. Y artesanía.

La concepción del museo no corresponde al sitio arqueológico, no solo porque es minúsculo en comparación a toda el área en la que están los templos, sino porque mantiene en almacén gran parte de los objetos y cuerpos hallados, no se exhiben. Su infraestructura es inadecuada por insuficiente y al 27/07/2024, luce muy descuidada por falta de mantenimiento, señalización y limpieza. Altamente recomendable contratar guía para el museo y el sitio arqueológico (especialmente), para comprender que incluso lo que se expone y preserva en el sitio, es una pequeña fracción de Chan Chan. Lo demás, está a merced de la inclemencia del sol, lluvia y viento, deteriorándose inexorablemente por descuido estatal y ciudadano.

Es una limpia playa de arena y agua agradable, el nombre de la playa se debe a la existencia del yacimiento de carbon del cual se servian los lugareños en la antiguedad, hay que decender aprox. 35 mts. Por escalinatas de concreto bien construidas para llegar a la playa , se puede observar fauna marina y aves guaneras, existen formaciones geologicas y cavidades producidas por la erosion marina y vientos , en estas se observan tambien restos fosiles de flora y fauna asi como capas de diferentes etapas de formacion geologica. Por el tema del decenso a la playa; no recomiendo estas playas para las personas con discapacidad en las piernas o de edad muy avanzada. Hay estacionamiento y seguridad

One more done from my Bucket List. The whole experience was amazing from the train ride to Aguas Calientes, the bus ride to Machu Picchu and the amazing 2.5 hour guided walk full of interesting history. We were very lucky that it didn't rain at all when we were there and although it was chilly, it was not very cold. It didn't feel crowded because they limit the number of people that go in. Take as many as you want for this once-in-a-lifetime experience.

Negotiated a private taxi to the site from Cusco as the bus fare for 5 people isn't that much of a difference to warrant the extra time travelling. Market selling all kinds of souvenirs at the entrance to the site. Tickets should be purchased in Cusco prior to your visit. Easiest option is to buy them as part of a package at Sacsayhuaman, the fabulous Incan and Pre-Incan site in the hills above Cusco. The site is fairly compact but you should allow at least 3 hours for your visit. The site is not suitable for anyone with walking difficulties. There are marked pathways to follow and handrails to help those people who struggle with heights. The Temple of the Sun at the very top of the site, made of Red Porphory stone speaks volumes as to the age of the Pre-Incan occupation. These stones are massive and incredibly weatherproof. The fact that you can only just make out the Chakhana engravings on the surface of these stones only confirms the fact that they have been in situ for thousands of years, not centuries as the guide books will tell you. The actual Incan ruins are also very impressive. The town itself is worth an overnight stay with accommodation and plenty of places to eat. You can, as an option, catch the tourist train from Cusco to Ollantaytambo as it's on the line to Machu Picchu. Toilet facilities are available inside the site. Child friendly enough but keep an eye on them as some of the drops are sheer.

Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

The archaeological site itself is quite impressive—a remarkable piece of history right in the middle of Lima. The restoration efforts are commendable, and it’s fascinating to see such an ancient place surrounded by the modern city. However, my overall experience was mixed. • Tour Experience: Upon arrival, you’re required to join a guided tour (self-exploration isn’t allowed), which lasts about an hour and costs 15 soles. The tour was fairly basic, not particularly engaging or inviting for questions. I visited around 2–3 pm in January, and it was extremely hot with little to no shade, which made the experience uncomfortable. Additionally, the final viewpoint was inaccessible due to ongoing restoration as well as the museum, which was a letdown. • Animals: One major disappointment was the treatment of the animals on-site. There are llamas and guinea pigs that you can see towards the end of the tour, but they’re kept in small, barren enclosures with no real diversity in their surroundings—just sand and cement. It was disheartening to see, and I wish they were given more space and a more natural, enriched environment. • Additional Highlights: On a positive note, I appreciated seeing the local herbs and plants displayed at the end of the tour, which added a nice touch. Overall, while the site itself is worth visiting for its historical significance, I recommend managing your expectations regarding the tour. Also, I hope the management improves conditions for the animals in the future—it really detracted from the experience.

text_clean: We love the Nazca Lines, a series of ancient geoglyphs located in the Nazca Desert of southern Peru. So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..

Whilst I appreciate the effort to recreate the impressive original state, the sense of authenticity has been lost. The information provided on the (mandatory) tour was lackluster. I would have liked to learn more information about the history, the architecture, the building techniques, the lima culture, instead of the plants the staff have grown in the back garden and the dogs and llamas on site. I also would have loved to walk around and explore myself. But sadly it's not permitted. Luckily the tour was only 1 hour.

A must-do if you are staying in Cusco and looking for something to explore! It’s a short and inexpensive taxi ride from the city. Be sure to bring water, sunscreen, and a hat—I got burnt even with sunscreen! You can easily spend half a day here enjoying the beautiful scenery and relaxing atmosphere. A large group of alpacas roamed around when we visited, which was a fun sight. Don't miss the rock slide at the back of the park and the cave walk-through! The slide is surprisingly fast but so much fun—even for adults! We didn’t use a guide, but there were several available at the entrance for a fee. Make sure to bring soles for payment. You can also purchase a multi-day or multi-park pass to save money if you plan to visit other sites. After visiting, you can either walk a scenic trail back to Cusco or take a taxi. Cristo Blanco is nearby and just a short hike away if you want to add another stop. Overall, this place was definitely worth visiting—peaceful with stunning views.

Machu Picchu was probably the most beautiful place I have ever seen. The views are absolutely breathtaking, but what makes this place truly unforgettable is the atmosphere. Standing there among the ancient ruins, surrounded by mountains and clouds, feels almost unreal. It is epic, mysterious, and peaceful at the same time. Every corner has something special — the history, the landscape, the silence, and the energy of the place all come together in a way that is hard to describe. It is not just a tourist attraction; it feels like a once-in-a-lifetime experience. For me, Machu Picchu was one of the most epic and magical places I have ever visited. Truly unforgettable.

El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: Cuál es la mejor temporada para visitar la Huaca del Sol y la Luna? Se puede visitar durante todo el año. Pero, se tiene que tener en cuenta que durante los meses de enero a marzo el clima en Trujillo es bastante caluroso y durante los meses de abril a diciembre es más fresco.Está ubicada al norte del Perú en el departamento de la libertad, a unos 5 km al sur de Trujillo. Se puede visitar este complejo arqueológico de lunes a domingo de 9:00 a.m. hasta las 4:00 p.m. Los tickets de entrada tienen un precio de S/. 10.00 para adultos. Qué llevar: Calzado cómodo, gorro para el sol, anteojos de sol, bloqueador solar, snack, agua y ropa cómoda y un poco de plata..😋 Es obligatorio tener un documento de vacunación con esquema completo.. En mí caso que vivo en tumbes puedo comprar paquetes online por el Tripavisor ó de lo contrario tomar un bus. Tumbes a Trujillo en Bus Oltursa es la principal empresa de transporte que cuenta con esta ruta en su itinerario, ofreciendo pasajes a precios económicos de 70 y 90 soles. Los buses salen desde el terminal., que queda en la calle principal. Con relación a los alojamientos, es muy simple, se busca por Booking que tiene muy buenas ofertas, claro está que hay que hacer las reservas con anticipación. Hay presupuestos y presupuesto todo depende como uno se organize y cuánto dinero quería gastar. SUERTE EN VIAJE!!

Ollantaytambo is a destination that combines historical intrigue with a bit of a physical challenge. The ascent involves a somewhat demanding staircase with quite a few steps. While it's not exceptionally high, be prepared for the climb. The entrance to Ollantaytambo is conveniently included in the partial ticket that covers four significant sites: Ollantaytambo, Moray, Pisac, and Chinchero. These are the typical inclusions in a one-day Sacred Valley tour, the ticket is not included in the tour and it costs 79 soles. If you're a student or a minor, you'll be eligible for the general ticket, which grants access to many more sites. However, if you're traveling with a group or on a tight schedule, you might not be able to fully take advantage of it. You can find tours starting at 100 soles or opt for private tours around 180 soles. I recommend purchasing the tour once you're in Cusco, as they tend to be more affordable than booking online, where they can go for around $70, which is almost double the cost. The main difference is the quality of the food. The tour I paid for was approximately 120 soles, and while the food wasn't exceptional, it was decent. Our guide recommended staying in ollantaytambo (which is almost at the end of the tour) to facilitate an early morning departure to Machu Picchu since it's much closer to the Ollantaytambo train station. The journey takes about an hour and a half, allowing you to arrive at Machu Picchu early and return later. In my case, I had already booked my round trip to Cusco, but I think is a good advise for people who is planning on going soon. Overall, Ollantaytambo has awesome views and an amazing history behind, I’m pretty sure you’ll enjoy, you definitely need a tour guide to go here, otherwise you won’t enjoy it as much. You can also buy souvenirs here.

CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.

Stepping into the Ollantaytambo Sanctuary is like entering a giant stone Sudoku puzzle built by celestial civil engineers. Terraces rise like ancient ziggurats, water channels still flow with supernatural precision, and massive boulders cling to mountainsides as if Newton had never existed. You climb , slowly, dramatically, gasping , not just from altitude but awe. Every step upward is a stairway to cosmic confusion. How did they move these stones? Why do the sunbeams hit exactly the right notch on the solstice? And why is that random llama staring into my soul? The Temple of the Sun? Part sun dial, part cosmic WiFi router. The granaries perched across the valley? Possibly Incan refrigerators, possibly alien Airbnb. 👣 Pro tip: Start early. Tourists swarm like guinea pigs on coca leaf. But by 7:30am, it’s just you, the stones, and the whispers of empires past.

Si no está listo para recibir turismo, por qué no lo cierran y se dedican a hacer las cosas bien.

I didn't even see a map of the park near the ticket offices/entrances. So self guided visitors can only follow the arrows to finish the tour. You must buy a partial (combo) tourist ticket for 4 historical sites, size a ticket for Saqsaywamam alone isn't available. But at the end of the self-guided tour, there is no signs pointing you to the next attraction included on the ticket. We had to ask at the ticket office since we didn't have a taxi waiting to take us there.

text_clean: Son las historicas líneas elaboradas por las culturas indígenas peruanas. Es posible hacer recorrido en avioneta y por tierra. Por tierra sólo se observan algunas de ellas, pero el recorrido es interesante, se puede acceder a varios miradores e ingresar al Museo de María Reiche, quien fue la mujer que las descubrió y dedico su vida a explorarlas y documentarlas.
El museo es excelente pero el ingreso a las ruinas esta prohibido y es un gran problema ya que uno asiste para ver la arquitectura del lugar.

I understand how Machu Picchu could once have been named one of the seven wonders of the modern world upon its discovery. A sacred place, suspended in the mountains and untouched by time — it must have felt like magic. But that time is long gone. Today, it’s overwhelmed by mass tourism. What truly broke it for me was seeing tourists cross safety barriers just to laugh and take selfies, ignoring the guards like misbehaving children. It made me question why I came at all. A place like this should be empty, inaccessible, preserved. I won’t return. I honestly hope it’s one day closed to protect its beauty and the peace it deserves. But in a capitalist world with millions of people all following the same paths — myself included — that probably won’t happen any time soon
We did a 2 days hike through the Colca Canyon. The views are absolutely amazing! To be honest we didn’t expect it would be such a long way up on the second day. 6 km with an elevation of 1000 meters.

Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures
Imperdible. Todo el cañón con múltiples atracciones para los que disfrutan de la naturaleza y de la antropología.

It costs 12 soles to get in. There are frequent tours in English. The guide was very good. At one time it was used as a motocross course. It will take them years to excavate the whole site. Worth the admission price but not spectacular.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo de las tumbas reales es muy bonito, antes de ingresar deben dejar sus cosas en la parte baja ya que no permiten ingresar celulares ni cámaras para tomar fotos o videos. El guiado dentro del museo está 30 soles, si conocen algo de la cultura mochica tal vez no sea necesario el guiado, ya que las personas que hacen el guiado les hace pasar muy rápido y no les permite disfrutar de todas las cerámicas, vestimenta del señor de sipan, los lugares, su cosmovisión, los entierros, los trabajos en orfebrería, sus actividades económicas, etc. Si son varias personas podrían juntarse y pagar el guiado de 30 soles, sino vayan tranquilo y disfruten del museo. Afuera del museo, pero dentro del enrejado pueden comprar en la parte trasera souvenirs de la zona que son cómodos y pueden tomarse foto, una vez hayan ido a recoger sus cosas donde las dejaron. Saliendo de ahí puede ir al museo de Brunning, que está a 4 cuadras exactamente. O tomar mototaxi hasta la tienda de kingkones San Roque, donde encontrarán todo tipo de dulces de la mejor marca que es SAN roque (en especial los antojitos que son kingkones bañados en chocolate como el chocan) , las otras marcas no las recomiendo. O sino pueden pagar un taxi o ir a la pista y tomar carro a la playa Pimentel para relajarse. Los taxis son baratos, pero deben saber negociar.

Saqsaywaman absolutely blew me away — the scale of this place is hard to comprehend until you’re standing right next to those massive stones. 🏔️ The precision of the Inca construction is incredible, especially knowing it was built without modern tools. The views over Cusco are stunning, and it’s a great spot to take a walk, explore, and really feel the history around you. It’s less crowded than Machu Picchu, which makes the experience more relaxed and enjoyable. I’m giving it 4 stars because there’s not a lot of information on-site unless you have a guide, and it can feel a bit underwhelming if you don’t know the history beforehand. But overall, it’s definitely worth visiting — especially if you’re already in Cusco and want something impressive without a full-day trip

Es una pena que desde niño haya conocido este lugar para hacer camping o a un hotel y luego a la reserva. No ha sido sino hasta ahora el lugar en que se habia convertido para miun lugar de paz, un lugar donde podía ir y orar, agradecer o llorar, completamente solo y en un contacto espectacular con la naturaleza, no todos pensamos igual pero parece que quieren construir mediante concesiónes las Playas, más vale prevenir antes de encontrar el problema y no poder hacer nada... Basta con que a una persona le interese y se use este medio para algo realmente importante. Gracias

Had I known they had animals locked up in tiny cages, exposed to the sun (animals that live in cold weathers) just to show you what animals these cultures were in contact with, I would never have paid the entrance. They could just use animal figures, like they do to represent humans throughout the whole site.

Es lamentable la atención en el Museo. Ninguno de los trabajadores sonríe ni es amable con excepción de el muchacho del cafe, la cantidad de vigilantes hace que parezca más una prisión que un Museo. Las instalaciones que le llaman la cabaña donde hay asientos para el público no está disponible y fui desalojado por los vigilantes a sabiendas que es un lugar para sentarse a dibujar y escribir.

If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

Such a nice walk...a bit tricky but worth it. And it's so simple to get your hands on a map online or in cobanaconde - I would recommend doing the route in the opposite direction to the tour groups and staying in San Juan (Roy's house was great and cheap). It was very simple and well sign posted to no need for a tour really unless you're short on time.

A must see in Cusco. Pretty much the most important ceremonial Inca site in Cusco. A walking distance from the center, though it is quite a hike up. It is better to take a transport there or to come down from Tambomachay, Puka Pukara or Q'enqo and then walk down from Sacsayhuaman to the city. Don't miss any location of the archaeological site, besides the main construction with the stones up to 12 tones each, there are natural slides on the volcanic stones area and also 2 fun tunnels to go through. Explore it with time and if you wonder whether a guide is worth it, this is the place for it.

Una joya arquitectónica de la cultura Chachapoyas. Puesta en valor recientemente y conjuntamente con el teleférico de Kuelap, son un excelente ejemplo de cómo la inversión convierte unas ruinas de gran valor histórico pero a veces olvidadas y sólo conocidas por unos pocos investigadores, estudiantes y aventureros, en un espacio cultural activo y que atrae a turistas nacionales y extranjeros convirtiendola en un polo de desarrollo.

Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.
Says it closes at 5:30 but actually closes at 3pm. Terrible for tourists who wouldn’t know this and only have one day to see it.
Another incredible place in Peru. It’s fabulous and so is the town. It’s just too bad Spain robbed Peru and incited comic book Christianity which had no measures of active reform and humanity.

The puya raimondii are cool, but the mystery of why they aren't blooming is interesting. The walk from the visitor center to the glacier was humbling. This place is a marvel, even though only 20% remains for visiting since the Spanish turned it into the Cusco quarry before it was completed. Seeing what ancient Incans were able to engineer without the benefit of the wheel is truly amazing to consider. It’s certainly beyond our means today.

Un lugar increíble que tienen que conocer sin dudar, una caída de agua espectacular, no es tan ondo, la temperatura del agua esta en su punto ni tan fría ni tan caliente, perfecto para ir en familia, están dejando entrar a niños la entrada cuesta 10 soles y tiene duración de 1 hora en el lugar. destination: Huacas del Sol y de la Luna
text_clean: Fantastic site, but really poor information for foreign tourists. You must enter the site as part of a tour and be ready to go at the prescribed time. This was communicated to me in rapid Spanish when purchasing a ticket, which I misunderstood as my slot time for entrance. The result was that I missed the start of the tour, had to chase the group through the site and totally miss the highlight (the exterior friezes) as the guards were hurrying me along, all so I could listen to a guide in a language I have fragmented understanding of. I certainly wouldn't expect people to speak English in this part of the world, but a simple sign in English at the ticket office explaining the requirement to be on a tour is a very easy solution!

Hicimos la ruta Cabanaconde - Llahuar, Llahuar - Sangalle y Sangalle - Cabanaconde. Es una ruta no tan turística y preciosa. En Llahuar nos alojamos en "Casa de Virgina", por 40 soles habitación doble y cocina riquísimo. En Sangalle tienes muchos hostels para elegir. Recomiendo el trekking, es un paisaje para recordar.
Museo pequeño, con réplicas de algunos artefactos interesantes y una maqueta gigante de la ciudadela. Hay un par de lugares para tomar fotos y algunos puestos de artesanías terminando el recorrido.

Es un lugar lindo, pero falta mejor señalización en los puntos a detenerse para visitar. Hay bastantes pu tos a visitar en el circuito como: pinturas rupestres, fósiles, centro de interpretación de la zona, pozo de aguas gasificadas, ojo de agua de siete colores (eso solo se aprecia si el sol a Sale y alumbra), Pastoruri (que esta en deshielo), etc. Es camino de pura trocha y a veces las curvas peligrosas porque son de 1 carril. Manejar con cuidado. Es mejor ir temprano para no encontrar mucha gente. La nieve ya no puede tocarse debido a a que se está deshielando, gracias al calentamiento global y contaminación con plástico en la zona que dejan los turistas.

An interesting fortress with lots of great views. The feeling of the place is powerful and the stone work is amazing. The only thing that was disappointing was the number of people that were there when I visited. (of course, I was one of those people.) The number of people made the climb difficult and slow due to the average fitness levels of the people, but the descent was even harder as a lot of people seemed afraid.

text_clean: The ground where the lines are drawn or scraped is covered with pebbles,where the iron oxide gives it its gray color.Shapes like straight lines,spirals,birds,triangles,monkeys,dogs,spiders,flowers etc. on the ground can only be seen when viewed from a very high height.Who created these forms still remains a mystery.According to archaeologists,it is highly likely that these forms were created between 200 BC and 700 AD.

Another great set of ruins worth checking out in The Sacred Valley. Supposedly bigger than Machu Picchu, though it didn't seem like it, but perhaps if you count all the agriculture terraces on the surrounding mountains that are uncovered and only noticeable with a sharp eye. Tranquil water fountains/baths, impressive stone fitting, and interesting theories/history from the guides make this an intriguing archeological site.

Excelente Museo!, Muy bonito museo sobre la ciudeadela de barro Chan Chan, puedes hacer un full day con la familia vistando chan chan y su museo; para mi la mejor para es el mapa en 3D que muestran como se veia en esos tiempos; al entrar te ofrecen guias apartir de 35 soles (9.26 $ US Dollars) al salir puedes sentarte a comer en unas mesitas y depende la fecha hay ferias que puedes disfrutar. If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!

Situated on the outskirts of the historic city of Ollantaytambo, the Ollantaytambo Archaeological Site stands as one of the most captivating and beautiful destinations in the Sacred Valley. Offering panoramic views and captivating walks amidst ancient Inca architecture, the site boasts remnants of the legendary Templo del Sol (Temple of the Sun) and terraces dating back to the Inca era, originally built for agricultural purposes. During the Inca period, Ollantaytambo served as a crucial 'tambo,' a resting place for travelers along the Inca Trail. It marked the final stop before continuing the journey to Machu Picchu, as well as the first stop upon departing from the famed citadel. Additionally, it functioned as a vital storage facility for grains and crops. Steeped in history, Ollantaytambo also played a significant role during the Spanish conquest, serving as a stronghold for the Inca resistance. During our visit, we were mesmerized by the site's magic, spending approximately 45 to 60 minutes exploring its wonders. While souvenir and collectible shops line the entrance gate, unfortunately, we didn't have the opportunity to peruse them.
Super interesting site, the guided tour was a bit sparse on details but still worth the visit. Take some protection from the sun though, very little shade.

The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

Te cuento que si ya tienes el boleto 🎟 que de ingreso porque primero fuiste al complejo arqueológico Chan Chan, presenta el boleto y la persona encargada arrancará un cuadrado que le corresponde o se identifica con el Museo. Si no tienes el boleto aún, no hay problema, te acercas a la ventanilla y pagas s/10,00 el cual te permite ingresar/visitar 4 lugares: 1) Conjunto Nik An Chan Chan 2) Huaca Arco Iris 3) Huaca La Esmerada 4) Museo de Sitio Chan Chan (también en la foto que subí puedes divisar los sitios) El museo de sitio es increíblemente Chevere asombroso, ni bien ingresas te encuentras con huacos representativos de la cultura Chimú, del lado izquierdo hay un mapa donde indica la expansión de la cultura, dónde se ubican las otras Huacas, hay reliquias, etc. Sigues el recorrido y verás una pantalla el cual te toma una foto de recuerdo, el Ministerio de Cultura del Perú 🇵🇪 lo obsequió al museo. ¡tomarte la foto es GRATIS! 🆓📸 No te cuento más sobre qué cosas hay en el museo porque no quiero arruinar la sorpresa que hay en uno de los espacios del museo :D! realmente me gustó el museo, entretenido y muy bien organizado y cuidado. La atención de los trabajadores es cálida en su recibimiento, están al tanto de lo que sucede. Recuerda ser un buen turista y cuidar el medio ambiente y el museo/completo arqueológico que visites. Demos el ejemplo.

I recently had the pleasure of visiting the awe-inspiring wonder of Machu Picchu in Peru, and it is no exaggeration to say that the experience surpassed all my expectations. This ancient Incan citadel, nestled amidst the breathtaking Andean mountains, proved to be a true masterpiece of human engineering and natural beauty. The journey to Machu Picchu is an adventure in itself. I opted for the classic Inca Trail, a four-day trek that winds through rugged terrain, lush forests, and ancient ruins. The trail allowed me to immerse myself in the history and culture of the Incas, and it built up my anticipation for the grand reveal that awaited me at the end. As I approached the Sun Gate and caught my first glimpse of Machu Picchu, my breath was taken away. The sight of the citadel perched high on a ridge, surrounded by mist and verdant slopes, was truly surreal. The sheer scale and intricacy of the stone structures left me in awe, and I marveled at the ingenuity of the Incas who built this masterpiece without modern tools. Exploring Machu Picchu was like stepping back in time. The well-preserved ruins tell a story of a vibrant civilization that thrived in harmony with its natural surroundings. Walking through the Temple of the Sun, the Intihuatana stone, and the Royal Tomb, I felt a deep sense of reverence for the history that unfolded within these ancient walls. The guides provided insightful explanations, bringing the site to life with captivating stories and legends. One of the highlights of my visit was ascending Huayna Picchu, the iconic peak that overlooks Machu Picchu. The climb was challenging but immensely rewarding. From the summit, I was treated to a panoramic view of the entire citadel, framed by the dramatic Andean landscape. The feeling of being on top of the world, surrounded by such beauty, was truly indescribable. The preservation efforts at Machu Picchu are commendable. The site is carefully managed to protect its integrity, and visitor numbers are regulated to maintain a sense of tranquility. This approach ensures that future generations will be able to appreciate and connect with this historical gem. Aside from the citadel itself, the surrounding area offers other attractions worth exploring. The charming town of Aguas Calientes, located at the base of Machu Picchu, offers a cozy atmosphere, delicious cuisine, and warm hospitality. After a long day of exploration, relaxing in the natural hot springs of Aguas Calientes was the perfect way to unwind. In conclusion, my visit to Machu Picchu was an extraordinary experience that will forever hold a special place in my heart. Its mystical allure, rich history, and breathtaking setting make it a must-see destination for any traveler seeking a truly transformative journey. Whether you choose to trek the Inca Trail or explore other access routes, Machu Picchu promises an adventure of a lifetime, leaving you with memories to treasure for years to come.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
Ante la majestuosidad de la Cultura Chachapollas solo queda admirar lo que el paso del tiempo no ha podido derribar....
